coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of hyaluronic acid with graphene quantum dot as a dual drug delivery system for cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Joseph
Lin, Jung-Hua
Yeh, Tseng-Yu
Zheng, Jia-Huei
Cho, Er-Chieh
Lee, Kuen-Chan
coredata.dc:description: With recent advancements in nanomedicine, there has been growing interest in developing drug delivery systems with multifunctional capabilities. In this study, we developed a novel dual drug delivery system by combining two drug carriers via positive and negative electrostatic interactions. First, we modified polyethyleneimine (PEI) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to create a positively charged particle (GPI) with high drug loading efficiency and good dispersibility. Second, we embedded the pyrenebutyric acid structure in hyaluronic acid (HA) through EDC/NHS cross-linking and used TAK-632 as the hydrophobic drug to create negatively charged particles (HANPs) with hydrogel-like properties and the CD44 receptor. After the two components were constructed, the increase in the particle charge and simultaneous delivery of both drugs synergistically enhanced the therapeutic effect of this strategy. Qualitative tests confirmed the successful synthesis of the drug carrier, while the potential of this system as a cancer treatment strategy was evaluated in HCT116 cancer cells (through MTT cell viability assays) and in vivo mice xenograft experiments. Our results demonstrated that the dual drug delivery system, HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX), had a significant inhibitory effect on the growth of cancer cells both in vitro and in vivo, suggesting that this system is a promising candidate for a new type of treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Dual drug delivery system
Electrostatic interaction
Hydrophobic drug

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro and in vivo assessment

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was partly supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3, NSTC 112-2221-E-152-001), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University, National Taipei University of Education (NTUE), and CPC Corporation, Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

This project was partly supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3, NSTC 112-2221-E-152-001), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University , National Taipei University of Education (NTUE), and CPC Corporation, Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of modification of HA and pyrene (a) UV–Vis spectrum of PB-EDA, HA and HA-PB (b) FTIR spectrum of PB-NHS, HA and HA-PB.
Characterization of HANPs(TAK-632). The SEM image of (a) HA-PB, before encapsulation and (b) HANPs(TAK-632), after encapsulation.
Characterization of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX). The TEM image of (a) HANPs(TAK), (b) HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) and (c) high magnification image of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX).
Characterization of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX). The ζ- potential of every stage in the formation of the drug delivery system.
Quantitative measurement of drug loading efficiency. UV–Vis spectrum of HA-PB, GPI, HANPs(DOX) and GPI(DOX).

MTT assay results of HANPs conjugates encapsulated different anticancer drug like SB-590885 (SB), TAK-632 (TAK), Glutaminase Inhibitor 968 (GI) and Curcumin (CU) over 24hr, 48hr and 72hr and respectively combination with GPI(DOX).

Tumor suppression evaluation of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) in mice xenograft model. Xenograft experiment was performed with Dox or HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) treatment as in the materials and methods. The size of the tumors in each group were examined (a), and the tumor inhibition rate was analyzed at the end of the experiment (b). The body weight of the mice in each group were monitored along the experiments (c). The representative pictures of tumors in each group were presented(d). HCT116 stands for the group without treatments. Dox stands for doxorubicin group, and HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) stands for treatment group.
Dual drug delivery systems synthesized through electrostatic attraction.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of hyaluronic acid with graphene quantum dot as a dual drug delivery system for cancer therapy

sa:affiliation.sa:address-line: No.134, Sec. 2, Heping E. Rd., Da-an District

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Dual drug delivery systems synthesized through electrostatic attraction.

With recent advancements in nanomedicine, there has been growing interest in developing drug delivery systems with multifunctional capabilities. In this study, we developed a novel dual drug delivery system by combining two drug carriers via positive and negative electrostatic interactions. First, we modified polyethyleneimine (PEI) with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to create a positively charged particle (GPI) with high drug loading efficiency and good dispersibility. Second, we embedded the pyrenebutyric acid structure in hyaluronic acid (HA) through EDC/NHS cross-linking and used TAK-632 as the hydrophobic drug to create negatively charged particles (HANPs) with hydrogel-like properties and the CD44 receptor. After the two components were constructed, the increase in the particle charge and simultaneous delivery of both drugs synergistically enhanced the therapeutic effect of this strategy. Qualitative tests confirmed the successful synthesis of the drug carrier, while the potential of this system as a cancer treatment strategy was evaluated in HCT116 cancer cells (through MTT cell viability assays) and in vivo mice xenograft experiments. Our results demonstrated that the dual drug delivery system, HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX), had a significant inhibitory effect on the growth of cancer cells both in vitro and in vivo, suggesting that this system is a promising candidate for a new type of treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has long been a prominent global disease, and the American Cancer Society estimates that there will be approximately 1.95 million new cases in the United States alone in 2023 [1]. Consequently, the treatment of cancer has emerged as a crucial area of research, involving various surgical and chemical approaches. However, traditional cancer chemotherapy faces challenges in terms of low specificity, inadequate biodistribution, and limited therapeutic efficiency. With the aim of overcoming these limitations, scientists are actively engaged in the development of a drug delivery system. Recent advancements in drug delivery systems (DDSs) have made remarkable progress in minimizing the adverse effects of chemotherapy by facilitating targeted drug delivery specifically to cancer cells while sparing normal cells from harm. These systems apply mechanisms for delayed and controlled release, thereby enabling the selective attack of cancer cells by drugs [2]. Among drug delivery systems, nanodrug carriers, such as liposomes, nanoparticles, and carbon nanomaterials, have gained traction due to their remarkable stability and ability to enhance penetration and retention effects (EPR effect) [3,4]. Carbon nanomaterials, including fullerene, carbon nanotubes [5], and graphene quantum dots, have already matured into drug delivery systems, among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which have excellent biocompatibility, bioimaging properties, and drug modification potential [6,7], have become the primary focus of research. Due to the quantum confinement effect, GQDs can be used to track drugs through photoluminescence and have reduced biological toxicity due to their small size [8]. Furthermore, the benzene ring structure of GQDs allows them to form stable noncovalent bonds with drugs with similar structures through hydrophobic-hydrophobic and π-π interactions, making them ideal drug carriers [9]. The outstanding specific surface area of GQDs further enhances their drug loading capacity, solidifying their status as a top choice among drug carriers [10].

In recent research, drug delivery systems based on graphene quantum dots have been developed using a wide range of molecules, including drugs, polymers, and nanoparticles [11–13]. Guang-Yu Lee et al. (2022) proposed a therapeutic strategy involving the modification of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with polymers, such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) and polyethyleneimine (PEI), to deliver the anticancer drug doxorubicin [14]. Although PEI-modified carbon nanomaterials significantly facilitate cell uptake and increase the ease of modifying targeting molecules, their cytotoxicity, which is caused by their positively charged surface, still limits their potential [15,16]. Therefore, another polymer with ultrahigh biocompatibility and a cancer cell targeting mechanism, hyaluronic acid (HA), has been extensively studied. HA is a primary research target due to its ability to target CD44 receptors on various cancer cells, as well as its excellent pharmacokinetic properties [17]. Interestingly, high-molecular-weight HA possesses hydrogel-like properties, making it easy to inject into subcutaneous tissue. Several studies have indicated that HA can also form nanoparticles through encapsulation when interacting with hydrophobic anticancer drugs, which makes it a good candidate for in vivo applications [18–21]. Nevertheless, it is noteworthy that the integration of graphene quantum dots into the HA system confers multifunctional disposal capabilities. For instance, the adhesion of dopamine has been utilized to facilitate the delivery of anticancer drugs such as doxorubicin or curcumin while simultaneously enhancing fluorescence. In addition, other researchers have employed selective ferrocene therapy for cancer, leveraging oxidative stress as a means of treating this disease with GQDs [21–24]. Hence, based on the evidence presented, it can be inferred that the combination of HA and GQD represents a promising and novel approach in the field of cancer treatment.

As research on anticancer drugs has advanced, there has been a growing focus on the phenomenon of multidrug resistance (MDR) in tumors. As previously mentioned, the issue of multidrug resistance (MDR) has seldom been addressed in the investigation of drug delivery systems that utilize graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hyaluronic acid (HA). To overcome MDR, several studies have developed dual drug-loading systems [24]. From a limited number of examples discussing this perspective, Pooresmaeil et al. reported that a combination of composite graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) can effectively codeliver anticancer drugs, such as doxorubicin and curcumin [25,52,53]. They developed a biocompatible platform by means of straightforward crosslinking and enhanced cytotoxicity against Hep G2 cells. Additionally, Yanmei He and her colleagues proposed a dual-drug delivery system that involves encapsulating doxorubicin with hyaluronic acid (HA) for precise delivery of multiple drugs to tumor-specific sites through cascade targeting [26]. Both of these approaches underscore the significance of dual drug loading and provide valuable insights into the design of therapeutic strategies aimed at mitigating the impact of tumor resistance.

Drawing on these studies, in this work we propose a novel composite material design for a dual drug delivery system that exhibits low toxicity and high loading capacity through noncovalent linking of two effective drug carriers. PEI-modified graphene quantum dots (GQDs) serve as carriers for efficient cellular uptake and facilitate the delivery of doxorubicin (DOX) via π-π stacking interactions. In the second component, hyaluronic acid (HA) is modified with pyrene to enable self-assembly and encapsulation of the anticancer drug TAK-632. Finally, these two components can be integrated into a novel treatment strategy utilizing positive and negative electrostatic interactions, thereby enabling cancer-specific targeting [13,27,28].

N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), N-(3-(dimethylamino)propyl)-N’-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 1-pyrenebutyric acid N-hydroxysuccinimide ester, and polyethylenimine (branched; PEI; average MW ∼ 25,000/Mn ∼ 10,000; shape: branched) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. 2-(N-Morpholino)ethanesulfonic acid (MES) was purchased from Calbiochem® Small Molecules (Thermo Scientific). Ethylenediamine was purchased from J.T. Baker. Dimethylformamide (DMF) was purchased from Showa Chemical Industry. Hyaluronic acid (MW: 600 ∼ 800 kDa) was purchased from Kikkoman. GO quantum dots (GQD, purity > 80 %, C:1mg/mL, size: <15 nm) were purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech. SB-590885 (purity > 98 %; MF: C27H27N5O2), TAK-632 (purity > 98 %; MF: C27H18F4N4O3S), and doxorubicin (purity > 98 %) were purchased from Cayman Chemical. RPMI medium, antibiotic–antimycotic, fetal bovine serum (FBS), and trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from Genetex.

We adapted the preparation procedure described by Lo, P.-Y et al [32] to synthesize HA-1-Pyrenebutyric (HA-PB) conjugates. First, we dissolved 17.4 mg of 1-Pyrenebutyric acid N-hydroxysuccinimide ester in 17.4 ml of dimethyl sulfoxide and added 3 μl of ethylene diamine. The mixture was stirred overnight. Then, we added 1 ml of the solution to varying concentrations of HA polymers, followed by the addition of concentrated EDC and NHS. The resulting product was stirred overnight and purified by dialysis (MWCO: 3.5–5 kD) against deionized water for one night. We then added 0.5 mg per ml of anticancer drugs into the HA-PB solution and sonicated the mixture at room temperature for 2 min, repeated three times. Finally, we filtered the mixture using a dialysis system overnight [29,30].

To form GQD-PEI (GPI), we synthesized GQDs with PEI shells by adding a solution of branched Polyethylenimine (1 mg/mL) to the GQDs-COOH suspension, followed by the addition of concentrated EDC and NHS. After stirring the mixture overnight, we purified the product using dialysis tubes (MWCO: 3.5-5kD) against deionized water overnight [13]. To load Doxorubicin (Dox) onto the GQD-PEI (GPI), we mixed 0.5 mg/mL of Dox with the GPI solution using sonication for an hour. We then removed unbound excess Dox by dialysis tubes (MWCO: 3.5-5kD) against deionized water overnight. The resulting GPI(DOX) complexes were stored at 4 °C.

After synthesizing the first two parts, a fixed volume is measured and GPI or GPI(DOX) is added to the solution of HANPs. This mixture is then stirred until it becomes uniformly mixed, which allows positive and negative charges to form a composite drug carrier, HANPs/GPI(DOX). Following this, further property analysis and cytotoxicity testing are carried out.

The synthesized nanomaterials were characterized by various techniques, including Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) using a Thermo NICOLET iS5 instrument, ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV/Vis) using a Hitachi U-5100 UV/Vis instrument, Field Emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) using a Hitachi S-4800 instrument, and Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) using a Hitachi H-7100 TEM operating at 120 kV. The ζ- potential was measured using a DKSH & Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZSP instrument with a 10mW He-Ne laser at 633 nm. FT-IR and UV/Vis spectroscopy confirmed the successful linking of 1-Pyrenebutyric acid to the HA backbone. TEM and FE-SEM were used to characterize the morphology of nanoparticles. The ζ- potential data provided evidence of an electrostatic interaction between the hybrid two different DDS.

We used doxorubicin as a model for loading the drug because it is common and has a benzene ring structure. The DOX loaded sample [ GIP(DOX) or HANPs(DOX) ] was dialyzed and the characteristic DOX absorbance at 490 nm in the supernatant was measured using an UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-5100). Loading capacity was calculated according to Eq. (1).
(1)
L o a d i n g
E f f i c i e n c y
( % )
=
C i
- C
r
C i
×
100 %
Ci and Cr are initial and residual DOX molecules concentrations, respectively [31].

To evaluate the in vitro drug release of GPI(DOX) and HANPs(DOX) nanoparticles, dialysis method was implemented in a simulated physiological condition. An aliquot of nanoparticles solution was packaged in dialysis bag (MWCO: 3.5–5 kD) and immersed in two separate PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4 and pH 5.5) as experimental and control groups. The pH value was adjusted by 1 N HCl. At each time interval, 1 ml of nanoparticles solution was taken out from the dialysis tube and then was determined based on absorbance at 490 nm with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

The HCT116 human colon cancer cells (American Type Culture Collection) were cultured in RPMI medium (Gibco) supplemented with 5 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1 % antibiotic–antimycotic (Gibco) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator.

A total of 6 × 103 cells per well were seeded in 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h. The cells were then treated with HANPs (drug) or HANPs (drug)/GPI(DOX) DDS for the indicated period of time before being analyzed using the MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenylte-trazolium bromide) assay (Goldbio). In brief, after the treatments, MTT reagent was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 incubator. The liquid mixture was then removed and replaced with DMSO (dimethyl sulfoxide) to dissolve the formazan. The concentration of purple formazan crystals was determined by measuring the optical density at 570 nm using an Epoch Microplate Spectrophotometer. Cell morphology was inspected using an IX73 Inverted Microscope.

Six-week-old nude mice were subcutaneously injected with 1 × 106 HCT116 cells into the right leg, and were randomly grouped with N ≥ 3 in each group. The mice's body weight and tumor size were measured three times a week. When the tumor size reached 100 mm3 on day 12, 1.5 mg/kg Dox or 100 μl 10-fold HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) dilutions were administered to the mice by intraperitoneal injection three times a week for four weeks. At the end of the experiment, the tumors were collected and measured, and mean ± SEM was used for body weight and tumor size analysis.

As shown in Scheme 1 , we developed a drug delivery approach consisting of two key elements. First, GQDs were modified with PEI via an EDC/NHS cross-linker to create positively charged particles, denoted as GPI. Then, the anticancer drug doxorubicin (DOX) was loaded in combination with these particles. Second, to introduce hydrophobic groups to hyaluronic acid, ethylenediamine (EDA) was applied as a coupling agent to facilitate the formation of HA-pyrene complexes (HA-PBs). This is achieved through the use of NHS-ylated pyrene and hyaluronic acid, followed by EDC/NHS cross-linking reactions. Then, HA-PB was used to encapsulate the anticancer drug TAK-632 to produce HA nanoparticles (HANPs). By intentionally engineering these two components to have opposite electrical properties, we successfully combined them into a drug delivery system [HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX)] capable of delivering two different drugs and then conducted in vitro cell viability tests and in vivo mouse tumor tests to evaluate its potential as a therapeutic strategy.

The success of the composite of pyrenebutyric acid (PB-EDA) and high molecular weight HA was confirmed by using UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, as illustrated in Fig. 1 (a). The analysis conducted showed that PB-HA exhibited two characteristic pyrene peaks at 340 nm and 350 nm, indicating successful conjugation of pyrene onto HA, possibly through cross-linking between the NHS ester of pyrenebutyric acid and the NH2 functional groups in EDA [5,32,33]. Furthermore, the Raman spectra of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) within the spectral range of 900–3000 cm−1 are illustrated in Fig. S2. The spectra exhibit typical graphitic characteristics, including the D mode (∼1315–1335 cm−1), induced by symmetry disruption at defects and edges, and the G-band (∼1595 cm−1), originating from in-plane C–C deformations [54]. The slight blue shift observed can be attributed to the functionalization of GQDs [9]. That is, the Raman spectra comprehensively represent the structure of GQDs in the carrier. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was utilized to identify bonding relationships within the composite materials [34]. In Fig. 1(b), a peak at 840 cm−1 is observed for HA-PB, which corresponds to pyrene butyric acid (PB-NHS) and can be attributed to the resonance structure of the pyrene benzene ring in the pyrene butyric acid. Additionally, characteristic peaks of HA in HA-PB are observed, at 1040, 1640, and 1411 cm−1, which represent the C-O-C stretching vibration of HA, the asymmetric bending of -C = O groups in HA, and the symmetric stretching of -C = O groups in HA, respectively [35]. In summary, a distinct peak at 1690 cm−1 was observed, which clearly indicates that the reaction proceeded through cross-linking of EDC/NHS to form an amide bond. This also confirmed the successful complexation of HA with pyrene butyric acid, enabling researchers to proceed to the next step of verification [36,37].

To further characterize the use of these composite materials for encapsulating anticancer drugs, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was utilized to observe the changes in surface morphology. Fig. 2 shows the changes in surface morphology before and after encapsulating the anticancer drug TAK-632 (Fig. 2(a), before encapsulation, HA-PB; Fig. 2(b), after encapsulation, HANPs(TAK-632)). On a larger scale, various high molecular weight forms of HA can be observed and they exhibit cavities that resemble hydrogel properties [38,39]. However, upon closer inspection of surface changes, it becomes apparent that relatively flat surfaces transform into spherical shapes through the encapsulation of the hydrophobic end and the drug TAK-632 to form nanoparticles [40]. The observed change in morphology can be attributed to the amphiphilicity of pyrene butyric HA (HA-PB), which leads to its self-assembly into spherical structures upon interaction with the hydrophobic drug TAK-632. This phenomenon is further supported by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The structures of HANPs(TAK) and HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) were found to be consistent with expectations, as demonstrated in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), respectively, via TEM. Fig. 3(a) clearly illustrates that the hyaluronic acid loaded TAK-632 particles were distributed within the aqueous solution. From the more detailed TEM image of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) (Fig. 3(c)), it is evident that the lighter area within the structure represents HA, while the darker particles dispersed throughout correspond to GPI. The size of the HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) particles is approximately 700 nm, and the size of the GPI particles is greater than that of commercial GQDs due to modification by the polymer PEI [14,41,42]. Fig. S3 presents a series of high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images (a to c), progressively magnified to reveal the distinct regions of higher electron density appearing as darker spots. These images comprehensively display the morphology of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) within the HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX), illustrating their distribution and structural characteristics.

A comprehensive measurement of the ζ- potential at each step was conducted to further analyze the validity of the design. Fig. 4 shows the changes in the ζ- potential of the drug delivery system at each stage [40]. Modification of GQDs with PEI changes the electrical properties from negative to positive, confirming the completion of GPI synthesis [43]. After loading GPI with DOX, the surface potential decreases due to the negative charge of doxorubicin [44]. Finally, when the HANP (TAK) solution was added, the original positive charge was completely neutralized, and the outermost layer of hydrogel-like HA was exposed. Hence, the ζ-potential (-43.2 mV) of HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) indicates that the dual drug delivery system is synthesized through positive and negative electrostatic attraction between the drug carriers, as previously reported [40,45]. In Fig. S4, zeta size analysis provides a rudimentary statistical representation of the surface particle size distribution, encompassing a range from 200 to 1400 nm. The most significant quantity is observed around 600 nm, which broadly aligns with the observations made in the TEM images (Fig. 3). Regarding the drug-carrying capacity, doxorubicin was applied as a fundamental model, and its effect was determined via UV–Vis spectrum analysis, as depicted in Fig. 5 . In comparison to those of the unloaded drug carrier material, both the GPI(DOX) and HANP(DOX) materials exhibited pronounced peaks at 490 nm. These results demonstrated that both HANPs and GPI possess significant drug-loading capabilities, with corresponding drug-loading efficiencies of 27 % and 57 %, respectively. Notably, the outstanding drug loading efficiency of GPI is attributed to the GQDs, which facilitate π-π interactions with the drug molecules and the remarkable drug loading capacity of HANPs can be attributed to the modification of pyrene on HA, resulting in the filling of its pi electron resonance orbital domain [7]. In summary, a dual drug-loading system [HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX)] was synthesized by introducing positive and negative electrostatic attractions to combine two carriers with high drug loading efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo assessment
ce:section.ce:para: The aim of this study was to assess the therapeutic potential of the dual drug delivery platform and determine the most effective anticancer drug for this system. Based on the results obtained from loading anticancer drugs onto HANPs, it can be concluded that HANPs exhibit outstanding drug loading capacity for a variety of drugs. Based on the results of loading anticancer drugs on HANPs alone, it is clear that HANPs exhibit excellent loading capacity for various drugs. This difference may be attributed to the conjugation of hydrophobic moieties between HA and the drugs [46]. After the addition of GPI (DOX), the overall cell activity significantly decreased, indicating that the electrical regulation of GPI increased cell uptake and demonstrated a strong toxic effect when combined with the other two drugs [47,48]. Interestingly, HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) exhibited less than 50 % cell viability within 24 h. We hypothesize that this difference is due to the full encapsulation of TAK-632 by HANPs, as shown by the TEM results, while other drugs have incomplete structures that reduce drug availability (Fig. S1). At 72 h, we observed a continuous increase in cytotoxicity, which may be attributed not only to the receptor CD 44-mediated effect of HA, but also to the excellent pharmacokinetics of HA, which allows for smooth tumor treatment through slow drug release [49,50].

To validate the tumor suppression capability of our hybrid nanoparticles in vivo, we conducted xenograft experiments in nude mice. Briefly, HCT116 cells were administered to nude mice, and when the tumor volume reached 100 mm3, the mice were treated with Dox, HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) nanoparticles, or HCT116 cells alone as a control, following the procedures detailed in the Materials and Methods section. The tumor size and body weight were regularly monitored during the experimental process. According to the results presented in Fig. 7 (a), the group treated with the dual drug delivery system exhibited significant suppression of tumor growth within one month. Notably, compared to that in untreated mice, the administration of doxorubicin alone resulted in a degree of tumor suppression (approximately 40 % suppression, Fig. 7b) but individual differences were observed (not even; in tumor size; Fig. 7d); however in the HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) treatment group, tumor growth was markedly suppressed (approximately 70 % suppression, Fig. 7b), and the tumors were uniform in size (Fig. 7d), suggesting a general, powerful, and stable anticancer effect of the HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) treatment [51]. During the experiment, we closely monitored and analyzed the body weights of the mice in each group. The results showed that the body weight of the mice remained stable at approximately 20 g throughout the study period (Fig. 7c), indicating the low toxicity of our HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) without causing any noticeable toxicity. These findings highlight the strong tumor suppressive ability of our HANPs(TAK)/GPI(DOX) nanocarriers in vivo.

In this study, we synthesized a dual drug delivery system using hyaluronic acid (HA) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) modified with polyethyleneimine (PEI) through positive and negative electrical attraction, respectively. This unique carrier structure enhances drug uptake by cells due to its electrical tunability and surface CD44 receptor, while its hydrogel-like properties improve its pharmacokinetics and efficacy in vivo. By carrying two different drugs, TAK-632 and doxorubicin (DOX), the system can effectively overcome tumor drug resistance and increase cytotoxicity. In vitro and in vivo experiments demonstrated the superiority of this system in cancer treatment. As nanodrug delivery systems continue to develop, these dual drug delivery systems undoubtedly offer a promising therapeutic strategy.

Joseph Lin: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Jung-Hua Lin: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Tseng-Yu Yeh: Investigation, Methodology. Jia-Huei Zheng: Formal analysis, Methodology. Er-Chieh Cho: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Kuen-Chan Lee: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was partly supported by the National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 112-2628-B-038-009-MY3, NSTC 112-2221-E-152-001), Taiwan. We also appreciate the supports from the Core Facility Center at Taipei Medical University, National Taipei University of Education (NTUE), and CPC Corporation, Taiwan, for the instrumental analysis and technical support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2024.100607.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Siegel, R. L., Miller, K. D., Wagle, N. S., & Jemal, A. (2023). Cancer statistics, 2023. CA: A Cancer Journal for Clinicians, 73(1), 17-48. https://doi.org/10.3322/caac.21763.
